GS2.headline: India Advocates for Equitable and Transparent WTO Reforms at Ministerial Meeting
GS2.explainer:
• India firmly reiterated its call for comprehensive reforms of the World Trade Organization (WTO) at the ongoing Ministerial Meeting in Geneva.
• The Indian delegation emphasized the need for a more equitable and transparent multilateral trading system that addresses the concerns of developing countries.
• Key demands included finding a permanent solution to public stockholding for food security purposes and curbing subsidies that distort agricultural trade.
• India also pushed for special and differential treatment (S&DT) provisions for developing and least developed countries to be preserved and strengthened.
• The discussions are critical for shaping the future of global trade rules and ensuring fair participation for all member nations.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: WTO was established in 1995, succeeding the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT).
Its main function is to ensure that global trade flows as smoothly, predictably, and freely as possible.
Ministerial Conference is the highest decision-making body of the WTO.
India is a founding member of GATT and WTO.
The issue of public stockholding for food security has been a contentious point since the Bali Ministerial Conference in 2013.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges faced by the World Trade Organization (WTO) in the 21st century and the necessity for its reforms.
Analyze India's stance on WTO reforms, particularly concerning public stockholding, agricultural subsidies, and special and differential treatment.
Evaluate how a reformed WTO can contribute to a more equitable and rules-based multilateral trading system that benefits developing countries.
Examine the implications of stalled WTO negotiations for global trade and India's economic interests.
Suggest ways in which India can leverage its position to advocate for its trade interests and promote fair global trade practices.
GS2.imageDescription: Logo of the World Trade Organization (WTO) against a global map.
GS2.trivia: The WTO's headquarters are located in Geneva, Switzerland.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: International Relations
prelimsSyllabus.area: Important International institutions, agencies and fora

GS2.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Reforms in Electoral Bonds Scheme for Enhanced Transparency
GS2.explainer:
• A Parliamentary Standing Committee on Law and Justice submitted its report recommending significant reforms to the Electoral Bonds Scheme.
• The committee advocated for greater transparency by making donor identities public or disclosing them to an independent body.
• It suggested lowering the minimum denomination of electoral bonds to encourage smaller donations and reduce the influence of large corporations.
• Concerns were raised about the potential for quid pro quo arrangements and the lack of a level playing field for political parties.
• The recommendations aim to address criticisms regarding the scheme's opacity and uphold the principles of fair and transparent electoral funding.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these.
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies.
prelimsPoints: Electoral Bonds Scheme was introduced in 2017 via Finance Act 2017.
Allows citizens and corporates to donate to political parties anonymously.
Bonds are issued by the State Bank of India (SBI) in denominations of ₹1,000 to ₹1 crore.
Valid for 15 days from the date of issue.

Only political parties registered under Section 29A of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, and which secured at least 1% of the votes polled in the last general election or assembly election, are eligible to receive electoral bonds.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives behind the introduction of the Electoral Bonds Scheme and the criticisms it has faced regarding transparency and democratic principles.
Analyze the recommendations of the Parliamentary Committee for reforms in the scheme and their potential impact on electoral funding in India.
Evaluate the arguments for and against anonymous political donations in a democratic system, considering the balance between donor privacy and public accountability.
Examine the role of money power in elections and how electoral finance reforms can contribute to a more level playing field for political parties.
Suggest comprehensive measures beyond electoral bonds to ensure clean and transparent political funding in India.
GS2.imageDescription: A ballot box with electoral bonds and a gavel, symbolizing parliamentary review of election finance.
GS2.trivia: India's Election Commission has consistently advocated for greater transparency in political funding and has raised concerns about electoral bonds.

GS2.headline: Government Initiates Consultations on Uniform Civil Code (UCC) Implementation Roadmap
GS2.explainer:
• The Union Government has commenced wide-ranging consultations with legal experts, religious organizations, and civil society groups to formulate a roadmap for the potential implementation of a Uniform Civil Code (UCC).
• The discussions aim to gather diverse perspectives on personal laws, seeking to harmonize them into a common framework while respecting cultural sensitivities.
• The Law Commission has been tasked with preparing a detailed report, considering various models and international best practices.
• The move signals a serious intent to address the constitutional directive under Article 44, promoting equality and national integration.
• While proponents argue for gender justice and secularism, critics raise concerns about minority rights and federal implications, making it a complex socio-political issue.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
prelimsPoints: Article 44 of the Indian Constitution states: 'The State shall endeavour to secure for the citizens a Uniform Civil Code throughout the territory of India'.
UCC is a Directive Principle of State Policy (DPSP), which are non-justiciable.
Personal laws cover matters like marriage, divorce, inheritance, adoption, and maintenance for different religious communities.
Goa is the only Indian state with a Uniform Civil Code.
The Law Commission of India has previously examined the issue of UCC.
mainsPoints: Discuss the constitutional mandate and historical context of the Uniform Civil Code (UCC) in India, highlighting its significance for national integration and gender justice.
Analyze the various arguments for and against the implementation of UCC, considering perspectives on secularism, minority rights, and cultural diversity.
Evaluate the potential challenges and complexities involved in drafting and implementing a UCC in a diverse country like India, with multiple personal laws.
Examine the role of the Law Commission and public consultations in building consensus and addressing concerns regarding UCC.
Suggest a pragmatic approach for moving towards a UCC, emphasizing gradual reforms and a focus on gender equality within existing personal laws.
GS2.imageDescription: A gavel and legal books, with a blurred background representing diverse Indian society, symbolizing the legal process for UCC.
GS2.trivia: B.R. Ambedkar, while drafting the Constitution, advocated for a UCC to promote social cohesion and gender equality.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Polity
prelimsSyllabus.area: Indian Constitution, Directive Principles of State Policy

GS2.headline: QUAD Nations Conduct Joint Naval Exercises in Bay of Bengal, Emphasizing Maritime Security and HADR
GS2.explainer:
• Naval forces from the Quadrilateral Security Dialogue (QUAD) nations – India, Australia, Japan, and the United States – concluded a major joint exercise in the Bay of Bengal.
• The exercises focused on enhancing interoperability, maritime domain awareness, and coordinated responses to regional security challenges.
• Key drills included anti-submarine warfare, air defense, surface warfare, and humanitarian assistance and disaster relief (HADR) operations.
• This demonstration of naval strength underscores the QUAD's commitment to maintaining a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific region.
• The exercises also aim to deter aggressive actions and ensure freedom of navigation in international waters, reinforcing regional stability.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: QUAD is an informal strategic forum comprising India, Japan, Australia, and the United States.
The Malabar naval exercise, initially bilateral between India and the US, expanded to include Japan and Australia.
The Bay of Bengal is a strategically important maritime region.
QUAD aims to work for a free, open, prosperous, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
HADR operations are a significant aspect of QUAD cooperation, especially in disaster-prone regions.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic significance of the QUAD grouping in ensuring peace and stability in the Indo-Pacific region.
Analyze the objectives and outcomes of joint naval exercises conducted by QUAD nations, particularly in enhancing maritime security and interoperability.
Evaluate the role of QUAD in countering rising geopolitical challenges and promoting a rules-based international order.
Examine the potential implications of increased QUAD cooperation for regional dynamics and the interests of other major powers.
Suggest ways in which QUAD can expand its cooperation beyond military exercises to address non-traditional security threats and promote regional development.
GS2.imageDescription: Warships from QUAD nations sailing in formation during a naval exercise.
GS2.trivia: The idea of the QUAD was first mooted by Japanese Prime Minister Shinzo Abe in 2007.

GS2.headline: Inter-State Council Meeting Discusses Fiscal Devolution and Centre-State Cooperation on Key Schemes
GS2.explainer:
• The Inter-State Council convened a crucial meeting to discuss mechanisms for fiscal devolution and enhance Centre-State cooperation on the implementation of major national schemes.
• Chaired by the Prime Minister, the meeting saw Chief Ministers from various states present their recommendations on resource allocation and federal financial relations.
• Deliberations focused on improving the efficacy of centrally sponsored schemes, ensuring states have greater flexibility in tailoring them to local needs.
• The Council also addressed issues related to GST compensation, disaster relief funding, and inter-state water disputes.
• The discussions underscored the importance of cooperative federalism in addressing national challenges and promoting balanced regional development.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure, devolution of powers and finances up to local levels and challenges therein.
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions.
prelimsPoints: Inter-State Council is a non-permanent constitutional body established under Article 263 of the Indian Constitution.
It is chaired by the Prime Minister and includes Chief Ministers of all states, Chief Ministers of UTs with legislative assemblies, and administrators of other UTs.
Its mandate is to investigate and discuss subjects in which some or all of the States, or the Union and one or more of the States, have a common interest.
The Sarkaria Commission recommended the establishment of a permanent Inter-State Council.
The Council's recommendations are advisory in nature.
mainsPoints: Discuss the constitutional provisions and significance of the Inter-State Council in promoting cooperative federalism in India.
Analyze the persistent issues and challenges in Centre-State financial relations, particularly concerning fiscal devolution and GST compensation.
Evaluate the effectiveness of centrally sponsored schemes and suggest measures to enhance states' autonomy and flexibility in their implementation.
Examine how the Inter-State Council can be strengthened to resolve inter-state disputes and foster greater collaboration on national development agendas.
Suggest reforms to the structure and functioning of the Inter-State Council to make it a more effective forum for federal dialogue.
GS2.imageDescription: Map of India with interconnected states, symbolizing inter-state cooperation.
GS2.trivia: The Inter-State Council was first constituted in 1990 following the recommendations of the Sarkaria Commission.

GS2.headline: India Calls for Review of Indus Waters Treaty Provisions Amidst Climate Change Impacts
GS2.explainer:
• India has formally initiated discussions with Pakistan for a review of certain provisions of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT), citing the impacts of climate change and evolving water resource management needs.
• The Ministry of External Affairs emphasized the need for modernizing the treaty's clauses to ensure equitable and efficient utilization of shared river waters.
• India highlighted concerns about Pakistan's alleged non-cooperation on certain hydro-power projects and the need for a more dynamic dispute resolution mechanism.
• While Pakistan has expressed reservations, India maintains that the review is essential for addressing contemporary challenges and preventing future water scarcity issues.
• The treaty, brokered by the World Bank in 1960, has largely survived decades of conflict but faces new pressures from environmental changes.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: Indus Waters Treaty was signed in 1960 between India and Pakistan, brokered by the World Bank.
It allocates the waters of the Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) to India and Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) to Pakistan.
India can use Western Rivers for non-consumptive purposes like hydropower generation with certain restrictions.
The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) is a bilateral commission for implementation and dispute resolution.
Climate change impacts on Himalayan glaciers affect river flows, adding new complexities to water sharing.
mainsPoints: Discuss the historical context and enduring significance of the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) in India-Pakistan relations.
Analyze the reasons behind India's call for a review of the IWT, particularly in light of climate change and evolving water management needs.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in renegotiating or modifying a long-standing international water treaty between riparian states.
Examine the implications of water scarcity and transboundary water disputes for regional stability and India's water security.
Suggest diplomatic and technical approaches to ensure equitable and sustainable management of shared river basins in South Asia.
GS2.imageDescription: Map showing the Indus River System flowing through India and Pakistan.
GS2.trivia: The Indus Waters Treaty is often cited as one of the most successful water-sharing treaties in the world, having survived multiple conflicts between India and Pakistan.

GS3.headline: National Green Hydrogen Mission Enters Critical Implementation Phase, Focus on R&D and Manufacturing
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Ministry of New and Renewable Energy announced the commencement of Phase II of the National Green Hydrogen Mission, focusing on scaling up domestic manufacturing and R&D.
• This phase targets establishing at least five major Green Hydrogen production hubs across India by 2030, leveraging renewable energy sources.
• Key initiatives include production-linked incentives (PLI) for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production, along with a dedicated fund for R&D.
• The mission aims to make India a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, reducing reliance on fossil fuels and achieving energy independence.
• Skill development programs and capacity building for the green hydrogen workforce are also a major component of this phase.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
Energy security
Infrastructure: Energy
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission launched in 2023.
Target: 5 MMT (Million Metric Tonnes) green hydrogen production capacity by 2030.
Expected investment: ₹8 lakh crore.
Key components: SIGHT program (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
Nodal Ministry: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE).
mainsPoints: Discuss India's potential to become a global leader in green hydrogen production and its implications for energy security and economic growth.
Analyze the role of government policies (PLI schemes, R&D funding) in fostering a domestic green hydrogen ecosystem.
Evaluate the environmental and economic benefits of transitioning to green hydrogen for various sectors like refining, fertilizers, and transportation.
Examine the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production, including infrastructure development, cost reduction, and technological advancements.
Elaborate on the 'Make in India' and 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' objectives aligned with the Green Hydrogen Mission.
GS3.imageDescription: Illustration of a green hydrogen production plant with wind turbines and solar panels in the background.
GS3.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the Universe, constituting roughly 75% of all baryonic mass.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Conducts Autonomous Landing Experiment of Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV-LEX)
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) achieved another milestone by successfully conducting the Autonomous Landing Experiment (LEX) of its Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) Technology Demonstrator.
• The RLV-TD, released from an Indian Air Force Chinook helicopter at an altitude of 4.5 km, performed precise autonomous landing on a runway, simulating a space return scenario.
• This experiment is crucial for developing India's future reusable launch capabilities, aiming to significantly reduce the cost of space access.
• The RLV-TD program is a step towards India's long-term goal of developing a two-stage-to-orbit (TSTO) fully reusable launch vehicle.
• Future tests will focus on orbital re-entry and hypersonic flight, progressing towards operational reusable launch systems.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
prelimsPoints: RLV-TD program aims to develop technologies for a fully reusable launch vehicle.
LEX is part of a series of experiments, following the RLV-TD HEX (Hypersonic Flight Experiment) in 2016.
The RLV-TD is a winged body, resembling a space shuttle.
Benefits of RLV include reduced launch costs, faster turnaround time, and increased launch frequency.
Other countries developing RLV technology include the US (SpaceX, NASA) and China.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of Reusable Launch Vehicle technology for India's future space missions and its implications for global space competitiveness.
Analyze how indigenous development of RLV technology aligns with India's 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' vision in the space sector.
Evaluate the economic and environmental benefits of reusable launch systems compared to traditional expendable rockets.
Examine the technological challenges involved in developing fully reusable launch vehicles, including materials science, navigation, and propulsion.
Suggest further steps ISRO can take to accelerate the development and operationalization of RLV technology.
GS3.imageDescription: ISRO's Reusable Launch Vehicle Technology Demonstrator (RLV-TD) landing on a runway.
GS3.trivia: The first fully reusable orbital launch system was NASA's Space Shuttle, which first flew in 1981.

GS3.headline: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs Launches New Framework for Sustainable Urban Water Management
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs has unveiled a comprehensive policy framework aimed at enhancing sustainable urban water management across Indian cities.
• The framework promotes integrated water resource management, emphasizing rainwater harvesting, wastewater recycling, and aquifer recharge.
• It mandates the adoption of smart water technologies, including IoT-based monitoring systems and AI for leak detection and efficient distribution.
• Cities will be incentivized through performance-linked grants to implement these measures and achieve water positive status.
• Capacity building for urban local bodies and public awareness campaigns on water conservation are also central to the new policy.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
tags: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
prelimsPoints: Jal Jeevan Mission (Urban) launched in 2021 to provide universal coverage of water supply.
AMRUT (Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation) focuses on urban infrastructure, including water supply.
Central Ground Water Board (CGWB) monitors and regulates groundwater resources.
Rainwater harvesting is mandatory in many states and cities.
The policy aligns with SDG 6: Clean Water and Sanitation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing challenges of urban water scarcity and pollution in India and the need for integrated management approaches.
Analyze the key features of the new policy framework for sustainable urban water management and its potential impact on urban resilience.
Evaluate the role of technology (IoT, AI) in achieving efficient water distribution, leak detection, and demand-side management in cities.
Examine the importance of community participation and public awareness campaigns in promoting water conservation and responsible water use.
Suggest policy interventions and financial mechanisms to encourage urban local bodies to adopt sustainable water practices effectively.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic illustrating various components of sustainable urban water management, including rainwater harvesting and water recycling.
GS3.trivia: Delhi, being a landlocked city, relies heavily on groundwater and water from the Yamuna and Ganga rivers, as well as canals from neighboring states.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Repo Rate at 6.5%, Signals Vigilance on Inflation Amidst Global Uncertainties
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) unanimously decided to keep the benchmark repo rate unchanged at 6.5% for the sixth consecutive review.
• The decision reflects the RBI's continued focus on withdrawing accommodation to ensure inflation aligns with the target while supporting growth.
• Global geopolitical tensions and commodity price volatility were cited as key factors influencing the cautious stance.
• The RBI projected retail inflation to moderate in the coming quarters but emphasized the need for vigilance against potential upside risks.
• Experts anticipate that the RBI will maintain its hawkish stance, with rate cuts unlikely until inflation shows a sustained decline towards the 4% target.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization, of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
prelimsPoints: MPC consists of 6 members (3 from RBI, 3 appointed by Government).
RBI's primary mandate is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Inflation target: 4% with a band of +/- 2%.
Repo rate is the rate at which RBI lends money to commercial banks.
A hawkish stance indicates a preference for higher interest rates to control inflation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the rationale behind the RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate amidst global economic uncertainties and domestic inflationary pressures.
Analyze the challenges faced by the Monetary Policy Committee in balancing inflation control with the objective of supporting economic growth.
Evaluate the effectiveness of monetary policy tools, particularly the repo rate, in influencing inflation and economic activity in India.
Examine the interplay between global commodity prices, geopolitical events, and domestic inflation, and their implications for RBI's policy decisions.
Suggest how fiscal policy can complement monetary policy in achieving macroeconomic stability and sustainable growth in India.
GS3.imageDescription: Reserve Bank of India building in Mumbai.
GS3.trivia: The first Governor of the Reserve Bank of India was Sir Osborne Smith, while the first Indian Governor was C.D. Deshmukh.

prelimsSyllabus.area: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources

GS3.headline: India Unveils 'Cybersecurity Policy 2.0' to Bolster Digital Defenses and Critical Infrastructure Protection
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) launched India's 'Cybersecurity Policy 2.0', a comprehensive framework to strengthen national digital security.
• The new policy focuses on creating a robust incident response mechanism, promoting indigenous cybersecurity solutions, and enhancing international cooperation.
• Key provisions include mandatory cybersecurity audits for critical infrastructure, establishment of a National Cyber Resilience Fund, and accelerated skill development programs.
• It emphasizes a multi-stakeholder approach, involving government, private sector, academia, and individual users in securing the digital ecosystem.
• The policy aims to address evolving cyber threats, including state-sponsored attacks, ransomware, and data breaches, ensuring a safe online environment for citizens and businesses.
gsSubTopics: Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: India's first National Cyber Security Policy was launched in 2013.
CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team) is the national agency for cybersecurity incident response.
The new policy aligns with the Digital India vision and aims to protect critical information infrastructure.
Focus on indigenous development of cybersecurity products and services.
MeitY is the nodal ministry for cybersecurity policy.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving landscape of cyber threats faced by India and the necessity of a comprehensive 'Cybersecurity Policy 2.0'.
Analyze the key provisions of the new policy and their potential impact on protecting critical infrastructure and ensuring data privacy.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing a robust national cybersecurity framework, including skill gaps, inter-agency coordination, and international cooperation.
Examine the role of public-private partnerships and individual cyber hygiene in creating a resilient digital ecosystem.
Suggest measures to enhance India's capabilities in cyber defense, threat intelligence, and digital forensics.
GS3.imageDescription: A stylized image representing cybersecurity with a shield icon over a digital network.
GS3.trivia: The Stuxnet virus, discovered in 2010, is considered one of the most sophisticated pieces of malware ever found, designed to target industrial control systems.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Internal Security
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Challenges to internal security through communication networks

GS3.headline: Project Tiger Completes 52 Years: New Census Data Indicates Stable Growth in Tiger Population
GS3.explainer:
• Project Tiger, India's flagship wildlife conservation program, marked its 52nd anniversary, celebrating over five decades of dedicated efforts.
• The latest All India Tiger Estimation report, released on the occasion, showed a stable and gradually increasing tiger population across the country.
• The report highlighted successful conservation strategies, including habitat improvement, anti-poaching measures, and community involvement.
• While the overall trend is positive, challenges such as human-wildlife conflict, habitat fragmentation, and poaching continue to demand attention.
• The National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA) announced new initiatives to strengthen corridors, manage meta-populations, and enhance genetic diversity.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Biodiversity and its conservation.
prelimsPoints: Project Tiger was launched on April 1, 1973.
India is home to over 70% of the world's wild tiger population.
NTCA is a statutory body under the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change.
The All India Tiger Estimation is conducted every four years, using M-STRIPES (Monitoring System for Tigers – Intensive Protection and Ecological Status) technology.
Global Tiger Day is celebrated on July 29th.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolution and success of Project Tiger as a model for wildlife conservation, highlighting its key strategies and achievements over 52 years.
Analyze the major challenges still faced in tiger conservation, including human-wildlife conflict, habitat loss, and climate change impacts.
Evaluate the role of local communities and indigenous knowledge in successful tiger conservation efforts in India.
Examine the ecological significance of tigers as apex predators and their role as an umbrella species for overall ecosystem health.
Suggest innovative approaches and policy measures to further enhance tiger conservation efforts and mitigate emerging threats.
GS3.imageDescription: A majestic tiger walking through a dense forest, symbolizing wildlife conservation.
GS3.trivia: India's tiger population has more than doubled since the launch of Project Tiger in 1973, from around 1,800 to over 3,000.

GS3.headline: India's First Indigenous mRNA Vaccine for Emerging Viral Threats Enters Phase III Clinical Trials
GS3.explainer:
• An Indian pharmaceutical company, supported by the Department of Biotechnology, announced that its indigenous mRNA vaccine candidate for a range of emerging viral threats has entered Phase III clinical trials.
• This vaccine utilizes cutting-edge mRNA technology, similar to those used for COVID-19, offering rapid development and scalability against new pathogens.
• The trials will assess the vaccine's efficacy, safety, and immunogenicity across multiple sites in India, involving a large cohort of volunteers.
• The development marks a significant leap in India's self-reliance in vaccine technology and preparedness for future pandemics.
• Researchers are optimistic about its potential to provide broad protection against diverse viral strains, including those with pandemic potential.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Health, hygiene and sanitation.
prelimsPoints: mRNA vaccines deliver genetic instructions (mRNA) to cells to produce viral proteins, triggering an immune response.
Unlike traditional vaccines, mRNA vaccines do not use live or inactivated virus.
Department of Biotechnology (DBT) is under the Ministry of Science & Technology.
Phase III trials are large-scale studies to confirm efficacy and monitor adverse reactions.
India has significantly boosted its vaccine manufacturing capacity post-COVID-19 pandemic.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of developing indigenous mRNA vaccine technology for India's public health security and pandemic preparedness.
Analyze the advantages of mRNA vaccine platforms over traditional vaccine technologies in terms of speed of development, scalability, and adaptability to new variants.
Evaluate the role of government funding and collaborative research in fostering innovation in India's biotechnology and pharmaceutical sectors.
Examine the ethical considerations and regulatory challenges associated with rapid vaccine development and deployment during health crises.
Suggest strategies for India to strengthen its 'Vaccine Maitri' initiative and contribute to global health equity with indigenous vaccine capabilities.
GS3.imageDescription: Microscopic view of mRNA strands and a vaccine vial, symbolizing advanced medical research.
GS3.trivia: The first mRNA vaccine approved for human use was for COVID-19, with emergency use authorizations granted in late 2020.

GS3.headline: Union Cabinet Approves Major Expansion of BharatNet Project to Connect All Gram Panchayats with High-Speed Internet
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved a significant outlay for the next phase of the BharatNet project, aimed at connecting all remaining Gram Panchayats (GPs) with high-speed optical fiber internet.
• This expansion focuses on enhancing last-mile connectivity, particularly in remote and underserved rural areas, through a Public-Private Partnership (PPP) model.
• The project will leverage existing infrastructure while deploying new fiber optic cables and establishing Wi-Fi hotspots in villages.
• The goal is to bridge the digital divide, facilitate e-governance services, promote digital literacy, and boost rural economic activities.
• This strategic investment is crucial for realizing the vision of Digital India and ensuring equitable access to digital resources across the country.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
prelimsPoints: BharatNet was formerly known as National Optical Fibre Network (NOFN).
Launched in 2011, aimed at providing broadband connectivity to all Gram Panchayats.
Implemented by Bharat Broadband Network Limited (BBNL) under the Department of Telecommunications.
Funded by the Universal Service Obligation Fund (USOF).
The project is crucial for e-health, e-education, e-governance, and e-banking services in rural areas.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the BharatNet project in bridging India's digital divide and promoting inclusive growth.
Analyze the challenges encountered in the implementation of BharatNet and how the new expansion phase aims to address them.
Evaluate the role of Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs) in accelerating infrastructure development and ensuring efficient service delivery in rural connectivity projects.
Examine the socio-economic benefits of high-speed internet connectivity for rural communities, including e-governance, education, and economic empowerment.
Suggest policy measures to ensure sustainable operation and maintenance of the BharatNet infrastructure and maximize its utilization by rural populations.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic depicting optical fiber cables connecting rural homes and villages, symbolizing digital connectivity.
GS3.trivia: India is the second-largest telecommunications market in the world with over 1.18 billion subscribers.

GS3.headline: India Joins Global Initiative for Climate-Resilient Agriculture, Focus on Small Farmers
GS3.explainer:
• India has formally joined a new global initiative aimed at promoting climate-resilient agriculture practices, with a specific focus on empowering small and marginal farmers.
• The initiative will facilitate knowledge sharing, technology transfer, and access to climate-smart agricultural inputs and financial resources.
• It emphasizes sustainable farming techniques, drought-resistant crop varieties, improved irrigation, and early warning systems for extreme weather events.
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare highlighted India's commitment to food security and farmer welfare in the face of climate change.
• This collaboration is expected to enhance India's agricultural productivity and resilience, contributing to global efforts against climate change.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.

Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, – different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues relating to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System- objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
prelimsPoints: National Innovations in Climate Resilient Agriculture (NICRA) is an Indian initiative.
Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) provides crop insurance against climate risks.
India is highly vulnerable to climate change impacts on agriculture.
Global initiatives often involve organizations like FAO, CGIAR, and various UN agencies.
Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 2 aims to end hunger, achieve food security and improved nutrition, and promote sustainable agriculture.
mainsPoints: Discuss the increasing vulnerability of Indian agriculture to climate change impacts and the urgent need for climate-resilient farming practices.
Analyze the key components and expected benefits of India's participation in a global initiative for climate-resilient agriculture, particularly for small farmers.
Evaluate the role of technology, research, and innovation in developing and disseminating climate-smart agricultural solutions.
Examine the challenges in transitioning to climate-resilient agriculture, including financial constraints, lack of awareness, and institutional support.
Suggest policy interventions and international collaborations that can strengthen India's food security and agricultural sustainability in a changing climate.
GS3.imageDescription: Farmers working in a field with modern irrigation systems, symbolizing climate-resilient agriculture.
GS3.trivia: India's agricultural sector contributes about 18% to the country's GDP and employs over 50% of its workforce.

GS3.headline: Government Boosts Startup India Seed Fund Scheme with Additional Capital and Expanded Eligibility
GS3.explainer:
• The Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) announced a significant enhancement to the Startup India Seed Fund Scheme (SISFS).
• The scheme will receive additional capital allocation and expanded eligibility criteria to support a larger number of innovative startups across diverse sectors.
• Focus areas include deep tech, rural innovation, and women-led startups, aiming to foster an inclusive entrepreneurial ecosystem.
• The modifications are designed to address funding gaps at the early stages of startup development and facilitate their journey from idea to market.
• This move is expected to further catalyze India's startup ecosystem, promoting job creation and economic growth.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: Startup India initiative was launched in 2016.
Startup India Seed Fund Scheme (SISFS) was launched in 2021 to provide financial assistance to startups for proof of concept, prototype development, product trials, and market-entry.
DPIIT (Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade) is under the Ministry of Commerce & Industry.
India is the third-largest startup ecosystem globally.
The scheme provides seed funding through incubators.
mainsPoints: Discuss the role of government initiatives like the Startup India Seed Fund Scheme in fostering entrepreneurship and innovation in India.
Analyze how addressing early-stage funding gaps can accelerate the growth of startups, particularly in emerging and deep tech sectors.
Evaluate the challenges faced by Indian startups, especially in rural areas and those led by women, and how policy support can mitigate these.
Examine the contribution of a thriving startup ecosystem to job creation, economic diversification, and technological advancement in India.
Suggest further policy measures and reforms to strengthen India's position as a global startup hub and promote inclusive entrepreneurship.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic showing a sapling growing from coins, symbolizing seed funding for startups.
GS3.trivia: As of 2024, India has over 100 unicorn startups (companies valued at over $1 billion).

prelimsSyllabus.area: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources

GS3.headline: Union Cabinet Approves New High-Speed Railway Corridor Project in Southern India
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet has given its approval for the construction of a new high-speed railway corridor connecting major economic hubs in Southern India.
• The project aims to significantly reduce travel time, boost regional connectivity, and facilitate economic development along the corridor.
• It will utilize advanced railway technology, including high-speed trains and modern signaling systems, adhering to international safety standards.
• The initiative is part of India's ambitious national infrastructure pipeline, focusing on upgrading transportation networks across the country.
• Land acquisition and environmental impact assessments are expected to commence shortly, with a projected completion timeline of five years.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
prelimsPoints: India's first high-speed rail corridor is between Mumbai and Ahmedabad.
National Rail Plan 2030 aims to create a 'future ready' railway system.
Indian Railways is the fourth largest railway network in the world.
Dedicated Freight Corridors (DFCs) are being built to decongest existing lines and improve freight movement.
High-speed rail projects often involve technology transfer and funding from international partners like Japan.
mainsPoints: Discuss the economic and social benefits of developing high-speed railway corridors for India's regional development and national integration.
Analyze the technological, financial, and environmental challenges associated with implementing large-scale high-speed rail projects in India.
Evaluate the role of such infrastructure projects in boosting employment, stimulating ancillary industries, and enhancing logistical efficiency.
Examine how the development of modern railway infrastructure aligns with India's vision for a 5 trillion-dollar economy.
Suggest policy measures to ensure timely completion, cost-effectiveness, and equitable benefits distribution from new railway corridor projects.
GS3.imageDescription: A sleek high-speed train speeding through a modern landscape, symbolizing advanced railway infrastructure.
GS3.trivia: The fastest train in India, Vande Bharat Express, can reach a maximum speed of 180 km/h, though currently restricted to 130 km/h on most routes.

GS3.headline: India's GAGAN System Expanded to Enhance Aviation Safety and Efficiency in South Asian Skies
GS3.explainer:
• The Airports Authority of India (AAI) announced the expansion and upgrade of the GPS Aided Geo Augmented Navigation (GAGAN) system.
• The enhanced GAGAN system will provide more precise and reliable navigation services, significantly improving aviation safety and efficiency not just in India but across the South Asian region.
• It enables aircraft to use GPS signals for precision approaches, reducing reliance on ground-based navigation aids, particularly in remote areas.
• This indigenous satellite-based augmentation system (SBAS) is a joint effort by ISRO and AAI, demonstrating India's capabilities in advanced aerospace technology.
• The expansion aims to facilitate greater air traffic management, reduce fuel consumption, and support the growth of regional air connectivity.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Infrastructure: Airports.
prelimsPoints: GAGAN is India's indigenous satellite navigation system, certified for civil aviation in 2015.
It provides accuracy and integrity necessary for civil aviation applications.
GAGAN is interoperable with other SBAS systems like WAAS (US), EGNOS (Europe), and MSAS (Japan).
It enhances the accuracy of GPS signals from 20 meters to 3 meters.
The system benefits sectors beyond aviation, including railways, maritime, and surveying.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India's GAGAN system in enhancing aviation safety, efficiency, and air traffic management, particularly in the South Asian region.
Analyze how indigenous development of satellite-based augmentation systems like GAGAN contributes to India's self-reliance in aerospace and space technology.
Evaluate the economic and environmental benefits of precise navigation systems for the aviation industry, including fuel efficiency and reduced delays.
Examine the challenges in integrating advanced navigation technologies into existing air traffic control infrastructure and training personnel.
Suggest ways in which GAGAN's capabilities can be further leveraged for applications beyond aviation, such as disaster management and precision agriculture.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic showing a satellite beaming signals to an airplane, representing the GAGAN system.
GS3.trivia: GAGAN makes India the fourth country in the world to have an operational SBAS, after the US, Europe, and Japan.
